planting.note: Direct sow after soil reaches 70°F; in zone 9 plant April–June for main crop, or as early as March under row cover. Jade okra is spineless and one of the most productive varieties per plant. Soak seeds 8–12 hours before planting to speed germination. In zone 9 fall plantings (August–September) often produce high-quality pods as temperatures cool.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Jade is a spineless, open-pollinated okra producing straight, medium-green, ribbed pods with a texture and appearance valued in Asian cuisine — the name reflects its appeal in Chinese and Vietnamese cooking where the mild-flavored, tender pods are stir-fried, braised, and used in soups. The medium-height plant produces generous yields of pods that hold their quality for harvest at 3–4 inches without becoming fibrous as quickly as some varieties. The spineless habit makes harvesting considerably more comfortable than thorny-spine varieties and allows the plants to be handled without protective clothing. In zone 9 Jade okra thrives in the long, hot growing season and can be planted in spring for an early summer crop and again in late summer for a fall harvest — two full production cycles are achievable in zone 9.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Okra Variety Trials
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Spineless Okra Varieties
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Okra Production Guide

planting.note: Direct sow after soil reaches 70°F; in zone 9 plant April–June for main crop, or March under row cover. Annie Oakley II is among the earliest okra varieties to produce, making it valuable for gardeners in northern zones. In zone 9 the earlier production means pods are setting before peak summer heat reduces pod quality; plant a fall crop in August for continued production into November.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile soil; Annie Oakley II is a hybrid, spineless, compact okra developed as an improved successor to the original Annie Oakley hybrid — the 1988 AAS Award-winning variety that introduced the compact, spineless, early-maturing concept that transformed home garden okra production. Annie Oakley II improves on the original with earlier maturity (48 versus 56 days), heavier production, improved disease resistance, and better heat tolerance for extended season production. The compact, 3–5 foot plant produces straight, uniform, deep green, 5-ribbed pods with excellent tenderness when harvested at 3–4 inches. The thornless, compact habit makes Annie Oakley II the best choice for gardeners with limited space, raised beds, and those who find full-size okra plants too large to manage easily. In zone 9 the compact habit is advantageous because plants fit more naturally into intensive garden beds without overwhelming neighboring crops.
sources: All-America Selections — Annie Oakley Okra Award Record
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Compact Okra Varieties
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Annie Oakley II Okra

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant to garden after frost danger has passed. In zone 9 start seeds in January–February and transplant in March–April. Ground cherries drop ripe fruits in their papery husks — harvest by collecting fallen fruits daily. In zone 9 ground cherries often self-sow and naturalize as short-lived perennials in mild winters.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Goldie is a named ground cherry cultivar specifically selected for larger-than-average fruit size and superior sweetness compared to standard ground cherry selections — the distinctive golden-yellow to pale orange fruits inside their papery lantern-like husks are significantly larger and sweeter than wild-type plants, with a complex tropical flavor combining vanilla, pineapple, and tomatillo notes with a sweet-tart finish unlike any other common garden fruit. Ground cherries (Physalis pruinosa) are closely related to tomatillos but used as a sweet fruit rather than a savory vegetable — excellent eaten fresh, baked into pies and cobblers, or made into jam with a natural pectin content that produces a firm set. The plant grows as a sprawling, somewhat sticky annual related to tomatoes, producing continuously from midsummer until frost. In zone 9 Goldie ground cherries produce abundantly and the plants often self-sow from fallen fruits.
sources: Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Goldie Ground Cherry
Cornell University Extension — Ground Cherry Varieties
University of Vermont Center for Sustainable Agriculture — Physalis pruinosa

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches 70°F; in zone 9 plant March–May for main crop and again in August–September for fall production. Tasty Jade is a long, thin burpless-type cucumber that produces abundantly on vigorous vines; provide a trellis or cage. In zone 9 the fall crop often produces higher-quality fruits as temperatures moderate after September.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Tasty Jade is an AAS Award-winning Asian burpless cucumber hybrid producing very long (10–12 inch), slender, uniformly dark green fruits with thin, tender, virtually seedless flesh and an exceptionally mild, non-bitter flavor. Unlike slicing cucumbers that can be bitter and require peeling, the thin skin and low cucurbitacin content of Tasty Jade makes it excellent for eating without peeling — just wash and slice. The crisp, sweet, mild-flavored fruits are ideal for fresh eating, salads, sushi accompaniments, and quick pickling. Tasty Jade's All-America Selections award recognized its superior flavor, production, and disease resistance compared to competing Asian cucumber types. In zone 9 the vigorous vines produce abundantly in spring and again in fall; summer production may slow during the hottest weeks of July–August but resumes as temperatures moderate in September.
sources: All-America Selections — Tasty Jade Cucumber Award
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Asian Cucumber Varieties
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Burpless Cucumber Production

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; Capsicum baccatum species takes longer to germinate and matures more slowly than C. annuum. In zone 9 start in January and transplant March–April. Lemon Drop thrives as a perennial in zone 9 and 10 — cut back hard in late winter and the plant will regrow from the roots, producing earlier and more heavily in its second year.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Lemon Drop is a Peruvian aji pepper (Capsicum baccatum) producing small, wrinkled, bright lemon-yellow fruits that ripen from pale green through various shades of yellow with a distinctive fruity, citrusy, bright heat very different from the harsher burn of jalapeños or the floral heat of habaneros. The heat level is medium-hot (15,000–30,000 Scoville units) with a pronounced fruity, lemony flavor note that makes Lemon Drop one of the most culinarily versatile hot peppers — used in salsas, ceviches, hot sauces, and Peruvian-style pepper pastes where the citrus-forward flavor complements seafood and poultry. Capsicum baccatum species peppers are widely grown in South America and increasingly popular in North American specialty gardening. In zone 9 the perennial habit is a major advantage — the plant can grow to a small shrub 4–5 feet tall over multiple seasons, producing far more fruit than annual plantings.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Aji Pepper Cultivars
New Mexico State University Chile Pepper Institute — Capsicum baccatum Species
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Specialty Pepper Production
